cases.question: organizational Google Suite account
cases.expected_behavior: Answer should identify the OpenGov email/account setup policy and cite the source containing this exact phrase.
cases.answer: Per OpenGov, employees will be set up with an organizational Google Suite account, which provides access to email, calendar, and shared storage. Employees are required to use their organizational email for all work-related activities and not personal accounts. Additionally, employees must keep their availability updated on their organizational Google calendar and use it for scheduling meetings and recording events like vacation time or appointments. Per Made Tech, there is no specific mention of an organizational Google Suite account or related policies in the provided context.

citations.snippet: Email Accounts, Document Sharing & Password Sharing # Email Accounts, Document Sharing & Password Sharing We will set you up with an organizational Google Suite account. This will provide you access to all the basic necessities like email, calendar, and shared storage. Please use your organizatio...

Calendars # Calendars Please keep your availability updated on your Google calendar account associated with the organization - please don't use your personal account. Please record any meetings, vacation time, appointments, and anything else that your teammates might need to schedule around. Feel...

faithfulness.reasoning: Every factual claim in the answer is directly supported by the context. The context explicitly states that employees will be set up with an organizational Google Suite account, which provides access to email, calendar, and shared storage. It also specifies that employees must use their organizational email for work-related activities, keep their availability updated on the organizational Google calendar, and use it for scheduling meetings and recording events like vacation time or appointments. The answer also correctly notes that there is no mention of an organizational Google Suite account or related policies in the Made Tech context.

cases.question: only 1 person from a small team
cases.expected_behavior: Answer should cite Made Tech holiday guidance about team coverage when people are away.
cases.answer:
Per Made Tech, only one person from a small team (less than 4 members) may be away at any one time. For larger teams (5 or more members), the policy advises consulting with the Delivery or Team Lead to determine what works best for the team. There is no corresponding policy from OpenGov on this topic, so this rule applies only to Made Tech employees.

The difference is that OpenGov does not provide any specific guidance on the number of people who can be away from a team at the same time, while Made Tech has a clear guideline for small teams and suggests consulting leadership for larger teams.

citations.snippet: We recommend you check with your Delivery Lead whenever you join a new team. Our general guideline on this is that only 1 person from a small team (less than 4) may be away at any one time. For larger teams (5+) please refer to your Delivery or Team Lead to confirm what works best for your team....

faithfulness.reasoning: The answer accurately reflects the context provided. It states that only one person from a small team (less than 4 members) may be away at any one time, which is directly supported by the context. It also correctly notes that for larger teams (5 or more members), the policy advises consulting with the Delivery or Team Lead, which is also explicitly mentioned in the context. Additionally, the answer correctly points out that there is no corresponding policy from OpenGov on this topic, which is consistent with the absence of any such information in the context. All factual claims in the answer are directly supported by the context.

cases.question: How do I stop participating in the workplace pension?
cases.expected_behavior: Answer should cite Made Tech pension guidance about opting out through Made Benefits and timing around the cut-off date.
cases.answer: Based on limited information in our policies, you can opt out of the workplace pension at any point by updating the pension section on Made Benefits. If you opt out at the start of your employment, any initial deduction will be refunded if this is done within one month of the initial deduction. If you opt out before the cut-off date (on the 18th), it will be processed in the same month; otherwise, it will be processed in the following month.

citations.snippet: Pension Scheme We have introduced a much-improved pension offering for all employees (as of May 2022). The workplace pension scheme is run by Scottish Widows and follows a contribution matching model where Made Tech matches the level of contribution you decide to make. The pension contribution av...

faithfulness.reasoning: Every factual claim in the answer is directly supported by the context. The process for opting out of the workplace pension, including updating the pension section on Made Benefits, refund conditions for opting out within one month of the initial deduction, and the processing timeline based on the cut-off date (18th), is explicitly detailed in the provided context. No unsupported claims are present.

cases.question: What happens if I am off sick and nobody hears from me by late morning?
cases.expected_behavior: Answer should cite Made Tech sick leave procedures explaining the 10:30 AM absence report and emergency contact escalation.
cases.answer: Based on limited information in our policies, per OpenGov, there is no specific policy addressing what happens if you are off sick and nobody hears from you by late morning. Per Made Tech, if the Account Team has not heard from you by 10:30 AM, they will report you as absent without reason to the People Team, who will then attempt to contact you. If they are unable to reach you, they will call your emergency contact at 11:30 AM. The difference is that OpenGov does not specify a procedure for this scenario, while Made Tech has a detailed process in place.

citations.snippet: Sick Leave We recognise that it requires a lot of hard work to perform at the highest possible level and our mental and physical wellbeing is an important foundation for this. Beyond taking sick leave when you have a cold or flu we appreciate that you may need to take a day when you feel burned o...

faithfulness.reasoning: The answer is fully supported by the context. The context explicitly states that if the Account Team has not heard from an employee by 10:30 AM, they will report the employee as absent without reason to the People Team, who will then attempt to contact the employee. If they are unable to reach the employee, they will call the emergency contact at 11:30 AM. The answer also correctly notes that OpenGov's policies do not specify a procedure for this scenario, which aligns with the lack of such details in the provided context. All factual claims in the answer are directly supported by the context.

cases.question: When is payroll administered?
cases.expected_behavior: Answer should state that OpenGov payroll is administered twice monthly on the 15th and last day, with Friday processing if the date falls on a weekend.
cases.answer: Per OpenGov, payroll is administered twice monthly, on the 15th and last day of each month, via direct deposit. If either date falls on a weekend, payroll administration will occur on the Friday immediately prior. Per Made Tech, there is no specific information provided about payroll administration in the context given. The difference is that OpenGov specifies payroll dates and procedures, while Made Tech does not address payroll timing.

cases.question: What documents are needed for an OpenGov reimbursement request?
cases.expected_behavior: Answer should cite the OpenGov expense reimbursement policy and mention receipts, expense classification, and a short descriptive note.
cases.answer:
Based on limited information in our policies, per OpenGov, each reimbursement request must include:
1. An image or PDF file containing receipts for each expense.
2. A classification of each expense into one of the following categories: Travel - In Town, Travel - Out of Town, Conference, Food and Drink, Supplies, Subscriptions, or Event Hosting.
3. A short note describing each expense (e.g., who, what, where, when, why).

citations.snippet: What’s Covered The organization will reimburse pre-approved, work-related expenses. Any expenditure totaling more than $1,000—including cumulative expenses like software subscriptions—must be pre- approved by the Board of Directors and Executive Director. Sub-$1,000 reimbursable expenses which do...

faithfulness.reasoning: The answer accurately reflects the requirements for an OpenGov reimbursement request as outlined in the context. It specifies the need for receipts, expense classification into the provided categories, and a short descriptive note for each expense. All claims in the answer are directly supported by the context.

cases.question: What do I need to do on the first day I am sick?
cases.expected_behavior: Answer should distinguish OpenGov's simple team notification/work-from-home guidance from Made Tech's Slack/email/operations notification, HiBob recording, and escalation process.
cases.answer: Per OpenGov, if you are sick and need to take the day off, you should notify the team. Additionally, if you feel well enough to work, you are advised to work from home until fully recovered to prevent spreading illness in the office. Per Made Tech, on your first day of sickness, you must inform your team (via Slack, email, etc.) or the operations team if you are on the Chalet, as early as possible, ideally by the start of business. If no notification is received, the Account Team will report your absence to the People Team by 10:30 AM, who will then attempt to contact you and, if unsuccessful, will call your emergency contact by 11:30 AM. The key difference is that OpenGov focuses on notifying the team and working from home if possible, while Made Tech emphasizes early notification and outlines a specific escalation process if no communication is received.

citations.snippet: Sick Days # Sick Days Please notify the team if you are sick and need to take the day off. Even if you are feeling well enough to work, please work from home until you are completely recovered, to avoid spreading illness through our small office.

Sick Leave We recognise that it requires a lot of hard work to perform at the highest possible level and our mental and physical wellbeing is an important foundation for this. Beyond taking sick leave when you have a cold or flu we appreciate that you may need to take a day when you feel burned o...

faithfulness.reasoning: Every factual claim in the answer is directly supported by the provided context. The OpenGov policy specifies notifying the team and working from home if possible, while the Made Tech policy outlines early notification, escalation procedures, and communication methods. The answer accurately reflects these details without introducing unsupported information.

cases.question: How much notice do I need to give before taking time off?
cases.expected_behavior: Answer should contrast OpenGov's no-less-than-5-business-days permission rule with Made Tech's notice periods that scale from 3 days to 3 months depending on holiday length.
cases.answer:
Based on limited information in our policies, per OpenGov, employees must inform their supervisor in writing as far in advance as is reasonable for staff and project planning purposes, with a minimum of 5 business days' notice required for scheduling time off. Extended periods of leave must be discussed with the Executive Director. 

Per Made Tech, the notice required depends on the length of the holiday: 
- For 1 day: at least 3 days' notice
- For 2 days: at least 1 week's notice
- For 3-4 days: at least 2 weeks' notice
- For 1-2 weeks: at least 4 weeks' notice
- For more than 2 weeks: at least 3 months' notice. 

The key difference is that OpenGov requires a minimum of 5 business days' notice for any time off, while Made Tech specifies varying notice periods based on the duration of the leave.

We exp...

Taking Holiday We’re building a high-performance organisation that delivers great software products. We recog- nise that this requires a lot of hard work and that for our team to perform at their highest possible level, they need to live a well-balanced life and take regular time-out for rest, re...

faithfulness.reasoning: The answer accurately reflects the context provided for both OpenGov and Made Tech policies. It correctly states that OpenGov requires a minimum of 5 business days' notice for scheduling time off and that extended leave must be discussed with the Executive Director. It also accurately outlines the varying notice periods required by Made Tech based on the duration of the leave. All claims in the answer are directly supported by the context.

cases.question: Can I work remotely, and do I need to come into an office?
cases.expected_behavior: Answer should distinguish OpenGov's broad remote/work-from-home support from Made Tech's hybrid policy encouraging 2-3 office days a month and client-site requirements.
cases.answer:
Per OpenGov, employees are allowed to work remotely and the organization ensures a comparable work experience regardless of location. Employees must attend required meetings and be accessible during core hours (11 AM–4 PM local time) unless otherwise noted on their calendar (<file_path>#<chunk_idx>: work-schedule-policy.md#0).

Per Made Tech, employees can work remotely, including from home, coffee shops, or even outside the UK, as long as it aligns with team and client needs. However, client-facing team members must be able to travel to client offices as required. Additionally, group-facing and non-client-facing team members are encouraged to spend 2–3 days per month in a Made Tech office for purpose-driven activities (<file_path>#<chunk_idx>: hybrid-working-policy.pdf#0).

The key difference is that OpenGov does not specify a requirement for in-office attendance, while Made Tech encourages periodic in-office presence for certain team members.

citations.snippet: Our Schedule # Our Schedule Employees are expected to work 40 hours per week, but we are flexible on where and when that work gets done. Employees are expected to attend all meetings to which they have been invited as a "required" participant. Communication is vital to the success of our team; th...

Hybrid Working We believe in people working in the best way for our teams and clients - this may sometimes be in person, remotely at home, from a coffee shop or outside of the UK. The best outcomes are delivered by blending different ways and places of work - and we want to empower our teams to m...

faithfulness.reasoning: The answer accurately reflects the context provided for both OpenGov and Made Tech. It correctly states that OpenGov allows remote work without specifying in-office attendance requirements, while Made Tech permits remote work but encourages periodic in-office presence for certain team members and requires client-facing employees to travel to client offices as needed. All claims in the answer are directly supported by the context.

cases.question: What are the normal working hours and availability expectations?
cases.expected_behavior: Answer should compare OpenGov's 40-hour week plus 11 AM-4 PM local availability window with Made Tech's Monday-Friday 40-hour norm and formal flexible-working process.
cases.answer: Per OpenGov, employees are expected to work 40 hours per week with flexibility on where and when the work is done. Per Made Tech, employees generally work Monday to Friday for 40 hours per week, with flexibility to manage their day as long as team and client commitments are met. The key difference is that OpenGov specifies core hours (11 AM–4 PM) for accessibility, while Made Tech does not impose such a requirement.

But for a variety of reasons, people may need to work fewer than 5 days per week. A formal flexible working request is wh...

It also accurately describes Made Tech's policy of working 40 hours per week, Monday to Friday, with flexibility to manage the day as long as team and client commitments are met, and notes the absence of specific core hours or mandatory availability windows. All claims in the answer are directly supported by the context.

cases.question: Who should I report harassment, misconduct, or a serious workplace concern to?
cases.expected_behavior: Answer should contrast OpenGov's designated management/board officers with Made Tech's line manager, People Partner, people team, and formal issue routes.
cases.answer: Per OpenGov, employees should report harassment, misconduct, or serious workplace concerns to the Executive Director, Chair, Vice Chair, or Treasurer of the Board of Directors. If the response is unsatisfactory, they should report to another designated person from this list. Per Made Tech, employees are encouraged to report concerns to their manager, but if they prefer not to, they can contact the Head of People, Head of Operations, or use the confidential whistleblowing email (whistleblowing@madetech.com). The difference lies in the specific roles designated for reporting: OpenGov specifies senior board members, while Made Tech includes operational and HR roles as well as a dedicated whistleblowing email.

cases.question: How do the policies handle whistleblowing or reporting suspected wrongdoing?
cases.expected_behavior: Answer should cite OpenGov's reporting and non-retaliation route and Made Tech's whistleblowing policy with manager, Head of People/Operations, whistleblowing email, and external disclosure guidance.
cases.answer: Per OpenGov, employees who believe they have witnessed a violation, such as harassment, should report it to designated members of management (Executive Director, Chair, Vice Chair, or Treasurer of the Board of Directors). If the response is unsatisfactory, they should escalate to another designated person. Reports will be investigated confidentially, and no adverse action will be taken against employees who report in good faith (<reporting-violations-policy.md#0>). Per Made Tech, whistleblowing involves reporting suspected wrongdoing, such as fraud or health and safety risks. Concerns can be raised with a manager, the Head of People, or the Head of Operations, or via a confidential email. Whistleblowers are legally protected and must not face retaliation. False, malicious allegations may result in disciplinary action (<whistleblowing-policy.pdf#0>, <whistleblowing-policy.pdf#1>). The key difference is that OpenGov focuses on reporting violations like harassment to specific management roles, while Made Tech provides a broader whistleblowing framework for various types of wrongdoing, with multiple reporting channels and legal protections.

Any suspected wrongdoing should be reported as soon as possible. 2. This policy covers all employees, officers, consulta...

Protection and support for whistleblowers • We aim to encourage openness and will always support whistleblowers who raise genuine concerns under this policy, even if they turn out to be mistaken. • All colleagues are legally protected if they make a qualifying disclosure. • Whistleblowers must no...

The distinction between the two policies is also accurately described. All factual claims in the answer are directly supported by the context.

cases.question: What medical, dental, and vision premium split does the handbook describe?

cases.answer: Per OpenGov, the company pays 80% of the premium cost for medical, dental, and vision coverage, while the employee pays 20% through payroll deduction. cases.question: Is there a budget for home office equipment?
cases.expected_behavior: Answer should cite Made Tech Work Ready Budget only and describe the allowance for essential equipment.
cases.answer: Per Made Tech, employees have a £250 Work Ready allowance for essential home office equipment, provided they complete DSE training and assessment. Eligible items include monitors, ergonomic chairs, and headsets, among others. Per OpenGov, there is no specific mention of a budget for home office equipment, but office supplies are reimbursable if pre-approved. The key difference is that Made Tech explicitly provides a dedicated budget for home office equipment, while OpenGov does not.
